title: Child Custody in Bangladesh – Muslim Law Rights and Procedure
metaTitle: Child Custody Bangladesh – Muslim Law Rights & Court Process
metaDescription: Complete guide to child custody in Bangladesh under Muslim law — who gets custody, at what age, how courts decide, and how to file a guardianship petition.
heroIntro: After a divorce or separation, child custody becomes the most emotionally and legally charged issue for parents. In Bangladesh, child custody for Muslims is governed by Muslim personal law and the Guardians and Wards Act 1890. Understanding the rules — who gets custody, for how long, and how courts intervene — is essential for protecting both your rights and your child's welfare.
toc: Custody vs. Guardianship: The Distinction Under Muslim Law
Hizanat: The Mother's Right to Physical Custody
At What Age Does Custody Transfer?
When Can a Mother's Custody Be Forfeited?
How Courts Decide: The Welfare Principle
How to File a Guardianship Petition in the Family Court
Visitation Rights for the Non-Custodial Parent

sections.h2: Custody vs. Guardianship: The Distinction Under Muslim Law
sections.content:
Under Muslim law applicable in Bangladesh, two distinct concepts govern children after parental separation:

  • Hizanat (physical custody): The right to have the child live with and be cared for physically. Under classical Muslim law (primarily Hanafi school followed in Bangladesh), hizanat belongs to the mother up to specified ages, regardless of who caused the divorce.
  • Wilayat (guardianship): The legal right to manage the child's property, give consent to marriage, and make major life decisions. This generally belongs to the father throughout.

In practice, these two rights can coexist — the mother has the child physically, but the father remains the legal guardian. A family lawyer in Dhaka can help you understand which right is at stake in your situation.

sections.h2: Hizanat: The Mother's Right to Physical Custody
sections.content:
Under Hanafi Muslim law (which governs most Bangladeshi Muslims), the mother's right to hizanat is automatic upon separation or divorce — she does not need to apply for it. The father cannot remove the child from the mother's care during the hizanat period without court order.

Key principles:

  • The mother's right to hizanat applies regardless of who initiated the divorce or whether the divorce was contested.
  • The mother's right exists even if she remarries — with an exception for sons over 7 and daughters over the pubescent age under classical law, though courts now primarily apply the welfare principle.
  • The father retains the right of access (visitation) during the hizanat period.
  • The father must pay maintenance (nafaqa) for the children regardless of who has physical custody.

sections.h2: At What Age Does Custody Transfer?
sections.content:
Under classical Hanafi law as applied historically in Bangladesh:

  • Sons: Mother's hizanat ends when the son reaches 7 years of age.
  • Daughters: Mother's hizanat ends when the daughter reaches puberty.

However, Bangladesh's Family Courts do not apply these ages rigidly. The controlling principle is the welfare of the child under Section 17 of the Guardians and Wards Act 1890. Courts regularly grant continuing custody to mothers of children well past these ages if it serves the child's best interests — particularly for daughters. Age is a factor, not a determinative rule.

sections.h2: When Can a Mother's Custody Be Forfeited?
sections.content:
The mother's right to hizanat can be lost in certain circumstances recognised by Muslim law and Bangladeshi courts:

  • Remarriage to a stranger: Under classical law, if the mother remarries a man who is not the child's mahram (close relative), hizanat ends. Courts assess whether the remarriage is in the child's interest.
  • Moral unfitness: Proven immoral conduct, neglect, or abuse.
  • Residence far from the father: Moving the child to a distant location that undermines the father's access rights.
  • Conversion from Islam: Changing religion may affect custody rights under classical law, though modern courts apply it less strictly.

Forfeiture must be proven to the court — it is not automatic. Courts are cautious about removing children from a primary carer. Strong evidence is required.

Even when the mother has physical custody, the father remains the natural guardian of the minor child under Muslim law and Section 19 of the Guardians and Wards Act 1890. This means:

  • The father gives consent to the child's marriage.
  • The father manages the child's property and assets.
  • The father must pay maintenance for the child regardless of custody arrangements.
  • Major educational and medical decisions may require the father's consent.

The father can apply to the Family Court for custody if the mother's hizanat period has ended or if she has forfeited her right. The father must demonstrate that custody with him serves the child's welfare.

sections.h2: How Courts Decide: The Welfare Principle
sections.content:
Bangladesh's Family Courts apply the welfare of the child as the paramount criterion in all custody and guardianship disputes — overriding classical age rules when necessary. Factors considered include:

  • The child's age and gender
  • The child's expressed wishes (especially for older children)
  • Each parent's financial and physical capacity to care for the child
  • Moral fitness and character of each parent
  • Continuity of existing arrangements (disrupting a settled routine harms children)
  • Proximity to school and access to education
  • The child's relationship with siblings
  • Risk of alienation from the non-custodial parent

Courts also appoint a guardian ad litem or conduct welfare inquiries in disputed cases. A well-prepared welfare report supporting your position significantly strengthens your case.

sections.h2: How to File a Guardianship Petition in the Family Court
sections.content:
If custody is disputed or the non-custodial parent refuses to return the child, the procedure is:

  1. Engage a family lawyer to assess your grounds and gather welfare evidence.
  2. File a guardianship petition under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890 before the Family Court of the area where the child ordinarily resides.
  3. Apply for interim custody simultaneously if the child is currently with the other parent and you believe immediate return is in the child's welfare.
  4. Serve the petition on the respondent.
  5. The court may conduct a welfare inquiry and/or hear the child (if of sufficient age and maturity).
  6. Hearings and evidence.
  7. Final order on custody/guardianship, with a visitation schedule for the non-custodial parent.

Our family law team handles custody petitions from initial filing through to final orders and handles cross-border custody issues where children have been taken abroad.

sections.h2: Visitation Rights for the Non-Custodial Parent
sections.content:
The non-custodial parent (usually the father during the hizanat period) has the right to access and visitation — this is a right of the child as much as the parent. Courts regularly specify:

  • Days and hours of weekly or fortnightly visitation
  • Holiday and Eid access schedules
  • Vacation periods with each parent
  • Communication rights (phone, video call)

Unreasonably denying the non-custodial parent access can be raised before the Family Court as a violation of the custody order and may result in variation of the custody arrangement. Conversely, a non-custodial parent who misuses access — for example, by attempting to remove the child — faces contempt and potential criminal proceedings.

faqs.question: Who gets child custody after divorce in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Under Muslim law, the mother has the primary right to physical custody (hizanat) — for sons up to age 7 and daughters until puberty under classical law. However, courts apply the welfare of the child as the overriding principle and may extend or modify these ages.

faqs.question: Can a father get custody of a young child in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the mother has forfeited her hizanat right (through remarriage to a stranger, proven unfitness, etc.) or if the court finds custody with the father better serves the child's welfare.

faqs.question: Does the mother automatically get custody after divorce?
faqs.answer: For young children, yes — Muslim law gives the mother hizanat without needing to apply for it. The father cannot take the child without a court order during the hizanat period.

faqs.question: What happens if a parent takes the child abroad without the other's consent?
faqs.answer: This can constitute contempt of court (if there is a custody order) and potentially child abduction under Bangladeshi law. Apply immediately to the Family Court for a recovery order and alert immigration authorities.

faqs.question: Can a non-Muslim child custody case be filed in a Family Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Guardians and Wards Act 1890 applies to all children in Bangladesh regardless of religion. Non-Muslim custody cases are governed by this Act and the welfare principle.
